audience. Certainly, you're a fan
favorite of of my self as well. You're
on the very, very short list,
a list that features less than 10
fingers of people whose words that I I
generally listen to pretty regularly.
You know, a lot of there's a lot of
noise out there in financial market
media, but you're one of those folks who
provide really clean, accurate, and
actionable signals, man. So, I
appreciate you for that and and a
world-class education as well. You know,
I think you do a phenomenal job of
educating people of these market
structure dynamics
in a way that most people have not
really seen before. So, I just want to
thank you and congratulate you on that,
as well as what you guys are doing over
at Kai wealth. Talk to me about that.
What's going on there?
Well, first of all, incredibly kind for
you to say that, and coming from you,
who I also tremendously respect, I I I
take that,
you know, as a as a big compliment. So,
thank you.
Appreciate it.
What are we doing? So, Kai wealth is a
new venture started in
May. It's a a non-correlated wealth
advisory program. So, basically, if you
think about it,
you know, the majority of the world is
60/40 stocks and bonds, directionally
focused owning investment investing is
broadly thought of as owning an asset.
Our view is that you don't have to just
own assets. There's a better way
to kind of to invest, and it is heavy
alternatives. When I say alternatives,
most people think private equity,
venture capital, real estate,
commodities.
No, those are not alternatives in our
book. Those are all, in our view,
long only assets.
They may take away the mark-to-market
of the
you know, of the movement, but they are
still
private equity. They are still equity.
They are still A lot of these things are
very very correlated with the stock
market even if you don't see that
correlation. So our view is very much
you need more non-correlated
strategies investment. We avoid duration
risk and credit risk as well on the bond
side which I think is very important.
Wow.
Um, and so we're really focused on
you know, creating solutions for people
to produce better risk-adjusted returns
across their entire portfolio.
That's a phenomenal product, man. Uh,
few questions and and slap me on the
wrist and tell me to move on if I'm
encroaching on some some secret sauce
but
what is the sort of underlying
fundamental return stream that you're
sort of tapping into, right? If you're
not going to be long assets, you know,
what is what is essentially the the
strategy generating return from?
Yeah, so before I dive into this just as
a disclosure, right? I I you know, don't
take any of this as current investment
advice, right? You probably already say
it on the front of all of your stuff. If
you're interested, reach out and we're
happy to have a conversation and and
work with you.
That said, at Kai Wealth, we we are very
focused on kind of a three different
bucket of allocations.
One
instead of a bond allocation, we we take
a alternative approach to credit. We do
what's called yield stacking.
It's a much more efficient way to get
after-tax yields that are actually
better than you can get in the bond
market
while taking significantly less risk.
And and how do we do that? I could get
into the details today but essentially
what we do is called boxes in the
options markets which are a T-bill
synthetic T-bill. They really are It's
really arbitrage and they trade for 30
to 50 basis points higher. So that's an
extra incremental yield. It doesn't
sound all that sexy, right? At at the
start. But what you what you don't
realize is that's also tax advantage. So
instead of paying ordinary income tax,
you pay a blended 60/40, which gets you
about 15% lower taxes. And if you have
some tax relief on the form of a
deduction, it's actually lower than that
because it's a 60/40. So, you get the
ordinary first. A lot of details there,
but what I'm at what that amounts to is
after tax currently people are making
about 2 and 1/2% right at the risk-free
T-bill rate. And we get closer to 3 and
1/2 3.6% to start. So, that again, not
going to make anybody like all that
excited. You're making an extra 1.1
after taxes, but it's that's risk-free
to me. Sure, you're that's arbitrage.
That's tax arbitrage and that's that's
options kind of synthetic arbitrage. And
to get anything for free, especially
when you're increasing your risk-free by
50% is a huge deal. There are other
benefits. The second you have that in
your portfolio, you're getting 100%
collateral on any stacking any risk that
you take in the options market
afterwards. As opposed to a T-bill,
you'd only probably get 90% and then on
top of that you'd have to have a
complicated structure where you
essentially have a collateral account
then you're getting a collateralized and
bank's going to charge you for that.
There's a lot of things that make it
more difficult that then stack on top of
that. So, you're starting with a box a
structure in the options market that
gets you that T-bill and then that all
counts as as 100% collateral to then
stack to the risk to ladder risk
essentially the way you want to. And you
can create risk defined strategies that
have a statistical advantage because of
risk premium. And you can use risk
premium in the form of put spreads, call
spreads, all kinds of other structures
that can then generate a yield on top of
that. And you can do those at a very low
leverage. You know,
better than cash secured max. So, we do
a max defined. Our favorite is defined
risk of about 8 and 1/2%.
You know, you can do a back 30 years.
This is not just a back test. Like Uh,
it cannot lose more than 10%, but in
over a 30-year period hasn't lost more
than 8 and 1/2 and
because of the structure and then
and that can yield on average over time
about 4% over risk-free. So, and tax
advantaged as well. So, when you start
looking at the basic numbers are if you
start looking at an 8 and 1/4
you know, 4 and 1/4 right now risk-free
plus
you're getting about 30 to 50 basis
points better before tax
on these boxes plus
another 4%. All of a sudden you're
looking at 8 and 1/2% plus
and then it's tax advantaged which works
out to something like a 6 and 1/2%
after-tax yield
which then if you do turn into an
effective like what would that be, it's
closer to an 11% pre-tax return given
the tax advantage nature.
And you're doing this
for an 8 and 1/2 max drawdown risk,
non-correlated
non- no duration risk, no credit risk.
It's a

one sharp strategies that return 10% a
year that may draw down 15% every 2
years, like that sounds good, right? But

um now there's this

two.

to five.

And
um

risk-reward. Um and what people do is
they extrapolate the
the most recent like What are What are
we looking at at a 5-year track record?
At most a 10-year? 40 years is like
forever. Like that you People laugh when
you say, "Well, you're looking at a
120-year track record." But there's a
reason we do that, because what people
don't realize is that
um the last 40 years, up until 2 years
ago, um have been correlated one to one
with a
bond yield, a 10-year bond yield, going
from 20 to zero in a straight line,
essentially, right? Like the
You look at it and it it You literally
could draw just a line and it goes
straight down for 40 years.
That's not a coincidence. Uh you know,
uh it wasn't magic. Uh the the Federal
Reserve
ran a supply-side economic model for 40
years that essentially pushed markets
there. But people confuse correlation
for causation. It happens. It's it's
human nature.
And because they think 40 years is
forever, they think that's just how
things will go for the next 5 years, 10
years, 20 years, 30 years, 40 years.
Um but they forget that interest rates
can go the other way. And they never ask
the question, "What if they do?" Uh you
know, what does that mean, right? And I
think that's so critical. Um uh and and
I I I feel strongly, and we've said this
for quite some time now, uh you know, to
this point accurately,
that that
that that process um is not a a 1-year,
2-year process. And mean reversion,
which happens for structural reasons in
markets over longer periods of time, are
likely to to play out in the bond
market. And that has massive uh It's the
most important thing to outcomes in
equity markets as well as bond markets.
Um and if you take that view that there
is mean reversion to the longer-term
mean, um during periods when that
happens, uh we're not worried about a
0.35 sharp for stocks. We're point we're
we're we're concerned about a negative
right particularly in real terms
performance.
It's incredibly poor.
You've heard me talk about this, but
I'll bring out the specter of the 60s
and 70s 68 to 82. Why do I choose that
period? Because it's the last time we
saw over some extended period a secular
move in interest rates higher not lower
right last 40 years until 21 were
straight down. When's the last time we
saw interest rates go higher? We should
probably go look at that. We should
probably understand what the risks of
that look like. Well,
68 to 82 when interest rates went
secularly higher again. Three different
major
rallies and declines, but again if you
draw a line again for 14 years secularly
working their way higher.
When we saw that what happened to the
stock market? Well,
the stock market went nowhere in nominal
terms for those 14 years and in real
terms lost 70% of its value.
It's hard for people to imagine for 14
years markets losing in real terms 70%
of their value. It's not in people's
vocabulary.
I would
encourage people to think about what
that means for all the things that have
worked so amazingly well for the last 40
years not just their public equities and
bonds, but
what happens to private equity if you
get negative real returns that last over
a decade? Those mark to market losses
that they were
able to cut out of the volatility
picture from COVID that they were able
to cut out of the volatility picture
from 08 09 that they were able to cut
out of the picture from 2001 2002
because those only lasted a couple
years. They were just short-term crises
with V bottoms.
What happens if the market doesn't come
back in a meaningful way and that
declines are happen over a longer period
of time.
And in those situations you have to
realize that private equity, much like I
talked about the multi-strategy, right?
How how they generate the returns, um
multi-strategy is truly non-correlated.
It's offsetting it's all of all of its
exposure. Private equity is not. Private
equity is equity. It's diversified
equity. But what happens to that equity
factor risk if it happens over 10 years
when you're deploying 100% leverage,
which is what private equity is doing?
Well, private equity is public equity
leveraged two to one and then charged,
uh you know, high fees on and then not
marked to market and it looks great
almost like a diversifier cuz you don't
see the volatility. That works until the
volatility is occurs over a longer
period of time.
So, things like this all the embedded
leverage in the system, there's a ton
that is kind of locked up and hidden
away
um in banks and in private equity and
all kinds of private credit. You can go
on and and look at it all and it's all
bundled away and sat in a corner cuz
it's not marked to market. Um and people
say, "Well, it it'll always come back.
Look at the last 40 years. All of our
models say." What happens if it doesn't?
Yeah.
Important to note that and understand
the risk that people are taking and the
risk that's embedded in the system based
on recency bias.
And again, the argument isn't this is
not to scare people.
But we probably should look at the
possibility of bonds not going down
but also going up over some period of
time if you're truly going to manage
risk.

believe the odds are higher or better or

Yeah, I was about to say like if you if

doesn't want to happen.

both of those specific issues. One,
inflation,

macro picture quant topic a little bit,
is is not happening
is not something that the Fed can
control.
Um, it is something that is a function
of

within a political system
saying, "We are not okay with money
continuing to flow to the top."
Yeah.
Um, in a system where you have um
in a natural system, money money flows
to the top, right? Survival of the
fittest, right?
The strong get the resources and and
capture
uh the majority of the gains. And that's
why since time incarnate, right? Like
there've been kings and queens and
emperors and
um and serfdom, right? And and the
reality is
um
as human beings, we have a
overdeveloped or
frontal cortex, which helps us to
manipulate the world around us,
to control the world around us, but by
definition for that to happen, we have
to have a a well-defined sense of self,
right? To to manipulate the world, you
have to know you are a thing that is a
manipulating the world around you. And
that sense of self is something that's
very
relative to any other animal in the
world, no matter how you think about
things, is very specific to us. And that
sense of self
means I look at myself and I look at you
and I say,
"There must be some equality or some
fairness." That's not a natural system.
Your mother, your father probably told
you life isn't fair. That's true. The
life in the in a natural state is not
fair.
Very much not.
But we want
to have a societal contract where we are
individuals
and we are equal and where we should be
treated fairly. That's what America is
all about, by the way. It's kind of
freeing ourselves from the natural
world. In order to do that, there's a
natural entropy towards
survival of the fittest in the natural
system, right?
Mhm.
And and our founding fathers here in the
US knew that.
And knowing that, they created an
elaborate sense of a set of checks and
balances, right?
Uh to help uh avoid that entropy and to
create a system that allowed people to
be more fairly treated and and to to to
have freedom and not be subjugated um to
to one another by power. Um
but the system is not naturally set up
that way. And so, in a natural state,
um which is what the Federal Reserve was
created so you know, the right believes
in a free market economic system because
it's more efficient,
um which is true.
Um and and that system ultimately drives
unequal outcomes and dramatically so.
So, the Federal Reserve through monetary
policy, which is supply-side policy, um
just pumps more oxygen into the system,
more money. It's the same thing, right?
Totally.
And and if you pump oxygen more and more
into the system to help build more and
more, you know, well, in the Mesozoic
era, pump more oxygen in the air, what
happened? We got really big dinosaurs.
Totally.
Like you you pump more oxygen into the
you know, into the free market economy
and
um corporations and the powerful become
more and more powerful and they grow and
they create more technology and
globalization happens faster and all the
things, right? And that's what we've
done for 40 years. And that's GDP
maximizing. That's probably maximizing
towards technological
innovation
um and progress.
Yep. Corporate profits.
But the problem with that system is if
we believe as a people
in a system of fairness, it becomes over
time a dramatically unfair
and unjust, much like the real world is,
by the way.
This is not a political argument. I
don't want to sit here and, you know,
debate the the the politics of fairness
or or whether fairness should exist.
But these two things are diametrically
opposed, and these two things ultimately
are the two biggest pressures over time
that drive to historical cycles, the big
big macro cycles. And at the end of the
day,
you get absolute power corrupting
absolutely. You get
everything going to the top until it
gets to the let them eat cake moment,
right? And
you know, we've reached our let them eat
cake moment. That's where we are.
Inequality has grown dramatically in the
developed world over 40 years.
And you know, Gini coefficients have
gone from 0.35, which is a measure of
the statistical width of the
distribution of wealth, to 0.45, 0.46.
It's actually coming in a little bit as
we start to have more populist policies,
but it won't happen quickly.
Nope.
And and as those things happen,
the the system is less efficient. It
does all the opposite things of free
market economics. It it drives a demand
push economy, which drives money to
people, which then ultimately,
you know, drives
one-to-one velocity of money. Money that
goes to the top is essentially zero. You
know, at in the short term has an active
velocity of money, but relatively low,
and then eventually
turns negative because it, you know, it
money finds it going to corporations
finds its way
to to profit-seeking enter enterprises,
which reduces the inputs, which human
beings are a major input to the whole
process. So,
100%.
Um we've said this before, but, you
know, you know, during
um the last 40 years, we've had 1.75%
real GDP growth approximately,
uh, give or take. Uh, about 0.75 of that
has gone to median outcomes.
What happened to the other 1%?
It went to the top. If we were to say
that top that 1% went to the top 1%,
that's 100% per year,
right?
For 40 years.
Um, and and uh, there's uh, you know,
you think compound You want to do a
compounding exercise? Try compounding
100% uh, growth for 40 years. I mean,
that's an exaggeration of the scale and
size of this and some there is some
trickle down, but but but the forces of
supply-side economics are incredibly
powerful and especially when um,
done over that type of a period of time.
So,
Yeah, so that's
Um,
so, anyway, uh, I I could go on and on
for some time about this, but you but
you see how when you get to a point
where and it's a generational issue. I
think this is another big takeaway that
we've talked about, but people assume
it's just about wealth disparity and
expansion and reduction in a in almost
like a a vertical sense uh, between the
rich and the poor. But what people
forget too is that you can turn that
wealth disparity on its side
horizontally to generations, because
when people come out of college, they're
labor
uh, or high school and when they retire,
they're capital.
Yeah.
Um, and and over time people build
wealth. So, what's happened is over this
40-year period, which is two
generations, baby boomers
have become a greater and greater
they've been capital. They've benefited
dramatically from um, from this
supply-side economic model.
Meanwhile, the
millennials on down have have been labor
and have been the ones being suffering
through this uh, this issue. So, when
you see a generational divide in this
kind of the oh, boomers, you hear that
or you know, like the system's broken or
the angst. Where's the angst coming
from? It's not coming from the boomers,
it's coming from the millennials on
down. They're the ones who've been stuck
at home with mom and dad,
unable to afford a home, unable to pay
for their college education,
etc. Is it any surprise that something
like Bitcoin would come and be the you
know the essentially the the zeitgeist
for that generation? The zeitgeist Why
is that the zeitgeist of the generation?

where 0% money led to massive
technological innovation. So they think
technology, cuz they're in that 40 years
it's been true, solves everything.

bond yields. It sounds It sounds crazy.
But I'm going to show you walk you
through three charts in 30 seconds to
show you why it's actually not crazy and
I would argue you could potentially
sniff 6% uh a year in in 2025.
So, in our view, uh there's sort of five
key risks that are sort of materializing
around midyear, Q3, uh in our opinion,
that could potentially create some
significantly hawkish dynamics in the
market. One, we could see uh the advent
of tariffs that cause a too strong US
dollar uh that ultimately drains
liquidity and makes the capitalizing of
the US government more onerous than it
already is with the private sector
owning about 55% of marketable Treasury
securities in a year where we're going
to have a record 11 to 12 trillion
dollars of gross Treasury supply.
Uh so, number two, we are likely to see
a further deceleration in core PC
inflation uh in the first 4 months of
the year. Our model has core PC starting
to back up again uh in May, reported
until June, and and, you know, starting
to trend higher from there. Uh cyclical
low in core PC inflation that is
obviously inconsistent with 2% inflation
in terms of the Fed's target. Uh number
three, uh you could potentially have
have that cause a hawkish pivot uh with
respect to the Fed and their general
assessment that they're roughly 150
basis points above neutral from the
sector of their policy rate, complete
repricing of the forward rate curve, in
fact, introducing two-sided risk to the
forward rate curve for the first time
since, you know, kind of probably uh
first half of 2023. Uh and then uh you
also have a uh potential hawkish shift
in uh
in Treasury uh debt composition as it
relates to the US's net financing
policy. Uh Scott Bessent, uh who I know
well as a client for a long time, and
former fellow Yalie, uh been very
critical of our fellow Yalies uh uh net
financing policy with respect to our
preference for uh bill financing. So,
it's likely that we start to see an
increase in coupon supply uh on a
nominal and and and relative basis. Uh
and then finally, we have
what is on record for being, you know,
the one of the biggest uh years ever
with respect to global refinancing
demand. When you look at the lagged
growth rate of global nonfinancial
sector debt, you know, the kind of that
peak that growth rate peaks on a on a
four and a half, five-year kind of lag
to simulate the early wave of the
refinancing cycle. That peaks in Q3 of
this year. You know, you you can argue
over the specificity of that, but in
2025, we will have a record year for
global private sector and public sector
debt growth in terms of refinancing
need. So, if all those things sort of
happen around mid-year, you could be
talking about a
I want to say a dearth of capital, if
you will, that forces a significant
repricing across sovereign debt markets,
and that takes me to these charts,
which, you know, will take me way less
time to to explain. In this top panel,
we show 10-year term premium at 54 basis
points. And for those who don't
understand what term premium are, it's
the excess return you get from rolling
over short-term paper instead of taking
a liquidity risk in the bond market by
locking your money up for whatever that
tenure is, 10 years.
If you subtract the deviation from the
long-run mean of term premium, which is
mostly positive for, you know, 50, 60,
70 years,
if you subtract that deviation from the
long-run mean, you're talking about a a
fair value on the 10-year from a term
premium adjustment that's five and a
half percent. That's a significant
change from where we are today, which is
around four and a half percent. And in
our opinion, that makes sense because we
are in a world that has higher
structural inflation, as you and I
generally agree on. We can unpack the
reasons why, but we don't have time to
do today. And historically speaking,
when you've had higher inflation, you've
had higher realized volatility in
inflation, but you've also had a
persistent, you know, inflation
ex-positive excess inflation relative to
the Fed's target. So, ultimately, you
have higher volatility and inflation
pricing in the bond market, which
contributes to higher term premium, but
you also have structurally less demand
in terms of the Fed being able to use
its balance sheet to take down risk. And
so, if you sort of put those two things
together, it suggests that we're
probably going to continue to see this
hawkish repricing of term premium in the
bond market. It could accelerate
markedly, you know, sometime around late
Q2, early Q3, and then throughout Q3 in
the second half of this year. And if
we're wrong on all that, and we have a
deflationary boom on AI from
productivity, then the yield curve is
just too flat to account for that
anyway. You're talking about 28 basis
points wide in terms of the 3-month
10-year Treasury yield curve on a
long-run mean of about 150 basis points.
You know, you would subtract that
deviation from the mean and you're still
talking about 10-year nominal yields at
around 5 and 5.6% and a 10-year TIPS
yield that's somewhere around 3 and a
quarter. So, no matter how you slice and
dice it, too much supply, not enough
liquidity, potentially too high too high
inflation or
deflationary AI-driven productivity
boom, in our opinion, the bond market is
just mispriced and it's mispriced at
least by 100 basis points right now. And
I would argue, you know, somewhere
closer to 250 basis points.
